items.title: L’Assemblée nationale reporte les auditions publiques sur la Coupe du Monde 2026
items.description: La Commission de l'Éducation, de la Jeunesse, des Sports et des Loisirs de l'Assemblée nationale a annoncé, ce mardi 21 juillet 2026, le report des auditions publiques consacrées à la préparation et à l'organisation de la participation sénégalaise au Mondial 2026 de la FIFA. Initialement programmées à partir du mercredi 22 juillet, ces auditions sont finalement reportées à une date ultérieure, selon le communiqué signé par le président de la commission, l'honorable député El Hadji Gueye. Un réaménagement lié au calendrier parlementaire La Commission justifie sa décision par des contraintes liées au calendrier parlementaire et par la nécessité d'assurer une meilleure articulation avec les autres travaux en cours au sein de l'hémicycle. Elle tient toutefois à rassurer l'opinion publique : ce report ne remet nullement en cause l'opportunité, l'importance et la pertinence de ces auditions. Bien au contraire, la Commission affirme vouloir garantir leur bonne préparation et leur organisation optimale. Un nouveau calendrier annoncé prochainement Aucune date de report n'a pour l'instant été fixée. Les députés promettent de communiquer le nouveau calendrier en temps opportun, une fois les ajustements nécessaires effectués avec les différentes parties concernées. La Commission a par ailleurs tenu à réaffirmer son engagement en faveur de la transparence et de la bonne gouvernance dans la gestion du sport national, un rappel qui prend tout son sens à quelques mois seulement du coup d'envoi de la Coupe du Monde 2026. O.B.N

items.title: As AI reshapes education, Ghana’s future teachers are being told what technology cannot replace
items.description: Artificial intelligence is changing the way people learn, work and communicate. But as technology becomes more common in classrooms, future teachers in Ghana are being reminded that teaching will require more than digital skills. They will need the ability to understand people. That was the message from Bosco Aboagye, a lecturer at St Monica’s College of Education, who was the guest speaker at the launch of the Social Sciences Students Association (SOSSA) at Agogo Presbyterian Women’s College of Education (APWCE). Mr Aboagye told trainee teachers that while technology would continue to support education, it could not replace the human qualities at the heart of teaching. “Technology will always aid humanity. Technology has no ethics, technology has no empathy, technology has no morals,” he said. His comments came at a time when artificial intelligence tools are increasingly being used in education for research, lesson planning and assessment. But Mr Aboagye warned that future educators must ensure technology remains a tool that serves people rather than replaces them. “While AI can process information, it cannot replace human wisdom, human empathy, moral judgement, compassion or ethical reasoning,” he said. “Technology is powerful, but it remains a tool. It is the human mind that gives technology purpose and direction.” Understanding society before shaping it For Mr Aboagye, preparing teachers for the future starts with helping them understand the society they will serve. “The sole aim of our training here is to train the next generation,” he told students. He said teachers are not only responsible for transferring knowledge but also for influencing attitudes, values and the future direction of communities. “Understanding society begins with understanding people,” he said. According to him, society is constantly changing through culture, technology, politics, economics, education, religion and human interaction. “Society is dynamic. It is not static,” he said. He urged students studying social sciences not to simply accept the world as it is, but to ask questions about the challenges facing society. “Social scientists do not simply accept the world as it is. They ask questions,” he said. He said those questions should include why poverty exists, why corruption occurs, why some educational policies succeed while others fail, and how societies can become more peaceful and prosperous. Beyond certificates The guest speaker also challenged students to develop skills beyond their academic qualifications. He said employers and institutions increasingly look for people who can communicate effectively, work with others, solve problems and adapt to changing circumstances. “They seek individuals who can communicate effectively, collaborate with others, solve problems creatively, adapt to change, lead with confidence and manage conflict,” he said. He encouraged students to develop research skills and improve their ability to use computers and digital tools. “Today, whatever the lecturer will tell you, with a click of a button, you can find it and read more,” he said. He urged them to read beyond lecture notes and develop the habit of independent research. “As long as you want to remain in academia, you can’t live in academic society without researching,” he said. Why SOSSA was established The launch of SOSSA was driven by concerns about the growing social challenges facing Ghana and the need to prepare student teachers beyond academic qualifications. Head of the Social Sciences Department at APWCE, Collins Owusu Asante, said the department reflected on these challenges before deciding to establish the association. “We sat down as a department and we thought through it and we saw the need to bring this association to fruition,” he said. Mr Asante mentioned issues including flooding, corruption, family problems and difficulties graduates face in finding employment. “Students are completing school and they are having difficulties getting jobs,” he said. He said the department wanted students to develop the confidence to look beyond traditional employment opportunities. “How can we train our students so that they can even look beyond government employment so that they can do something for themselves?” he asked. He explained that SOSSA would provide opportunities for students to organise seminars, educational trips and engage with similar associations in other colleges. The aim, he said, is to help students gain broader experiences and prepare for life after graduation. Building teachers for Ghana’s future The Principal of Agogo Presbyterian Women’s College of Education. Professor Lydia Aframea Dankyi said the new association fits into the institution’s broader vision of producing teachers capable of contributing to national development. She said the college aims to become “an institution of excellence among the best in the country and beyond.” She added that the college is focused on producing teachers who are disciplined, innovative, dedicated, competent, committed and selfless. However, she cautioned students against allowing associations to create divisions within the college community. She said the different groups should work together towards a shared goal. “It is one college with one aim,” she said. She urged students to support one another regardless of which association is organizing an activity. “How can you collaborate and you stay aloof when it is somebody’s turn to perform?” she asked. She said student associations should promote academic excellence, leadership development and collaboration rather than competition. A platform for future educators For the leadership of SOSSA, the association is intended to help student teachers better understand the communities they will eventually serve. SOSSA President Mary Brobbey said the association would help students build stronger relationships with society and future learners. “The association is going to shape us and understand society as well and help us to build the rapport with the students,” she said. She said the group would contribute to shaping future leaders. Vice President Grace Owusu said the association would also promote positive values among students.

items.title: Wontumi appeal to challenge over 20 legal errors, including constitutional breaches – Atta Akyea
items.description: Samuel Atta Akyea, the lead counsel for the Ashanti Regional Chairman of the New Patriotic Party (NPP), Bernard Antwi Boasiako, popularly known as Chairman Wontumi, says the defence team has identified more than 20 grounds of appeal against his client’s conviction, arguing that the trial was marred by fundamental constitutional and legal errors. Speaking on JoyFM’s Top Story on Tuesday, July 21, Samuel Atta Akyea said one of the key issues the defence will raise is the trial judge’s decision not to refer constitutional questions to the Supreme Court for interpretation before delivering judgement. His comments come after the Minority in Parliament described the judgement against Chairman Wontumi as defective and questioned the legal basis for the conviction. “So far we’ve assembled no less than 20 grounds of appeal,” Mr Atta Akyea revealed. “The first one is a constitutional imperative that Her Ladyship was bound to make a reference to the Supreme Court for the determination of critical issues.” According to him, the case raised constitutional questions under Articles 130 and 19(11) of the 1992 Constitution, as well as provisions of the Minerals and Mining Act, which only the Supreme Court has the authority to interpret. Mr Atta Akyea argued that both the defence and the Attorney General advanced competing interpretations of the Constitution during the trial, making it mandatory for the High Court to seek guidance from the apex court. He cited the Akosah case, which he said establishes that where parties place rival interpretations on constitutional provisions, the matter should be referred to the Supreme Court. “Even the very authority that she cited in the judgment is aware rival meanings are placed on the Constitution by the parties,” he said. “So we said the manner of the charges and the way it’s been couched sins against Article 19(11) and therefore the Supreme Court should interpret it. The Attorney General said no.” Mr Atta Akyea maintained that by determining the constitutional questions herself, the trial judge exceeded her jurisdiction. “The court graduated itself into the Supreme Court to make a determination of constitutional matters. For me, that is a very fundamental error which can quash the entire decision because the power to interpret the Constitution is not vested in a High Court judge; it is with the Supreme Court,” he argued. He further contended that the charges against Chairman Wontumi failed to satisfy the constitutional requirement that criminal offences and their corresponding penalties be clearly defined. “It was not a question of statutory construction. It was a constitutional interpretation as to whether the offence, as formulated, met the requirements of Article 19(11), which says a person cannot be convicted unless the offence and its penalty are clearly defined,” he said. The former Akim Abuakwa South Member of Parliament described the judge’s refusal to refer the constitutional issues as a serious error that undermined the entire proceeding. “I believe it’s a very serious matter that the judge ignored. It seems to me that the judge was in an ambulance mood to convict the guy. “I do not see how any High Court judge will ignore serious and consequential constitutional interpretation and go ahead with the matter,” he said. Mr Atta Akyea said the defence is confident the Court of Appeal will carefully examine the constitutional and procedural issues raised and determine whether the conviction can stand.

items.title: Boulgou/Zoaga : La maternité du CSPS réfectionnée grâce à un opérateur économique
items.description: Boulgou/Zoaga : La maternité du CSPS réfectionnée grâce à un opérateur économique Zoaga, 17 juil. 2026 (AIB) – La commune de Zoaga a vibré, le jeudi 17 juillet 2026, au rythme de la cérémonie de réception des travaux de réfection de la maternité du Centre de santé et de promotion sociale (CSPS). Les travaux ont été entièrement financés par le PDG de la Société Pissa Gouba Gnassou Koum (PSGGK), El Hadj Ousmane Gouba. La cérémonie a été présidée par le président de la délégation spéciale (PDS) de la commune de Zoaga. Elle a connu une forte mobilisation des autorités administratives, coutumières et religieuses, ainsi que des femmes de la localité. Dans son mot de bienvenue, le représentant du chef de canton de Zoaga, le Saam Naaba, a remercié l'ensemble des autorités présentes. Il a également exprimé sa gratitude au donateur qui a fait de la réfection de la maternité une priorité. Le major et chef de poste du CSPS de Zoaga, Idrissa Boulou, a salué ce geste salvateur. Pour lui, cette réfection est un acte bénéfique pour toute la commune. « Cette réfection va améliorer les conditions de travail des agents du CSPS et faciliter les accouchements des femmes », a-t-il déclaré. La coordonnatrice des femmes de Zoaga a, au nom des bénéficiaires, témoigné sa reconnaissance au bienfaiteur. Prenant la parole, le PDS de Zoaga a remercié de vive voix le donateur. Il a saisi l'occasion pour inviter les filles et fils de la commune à emboîter le pas du donateur. « On ne peut pas tout attendre de l'Etat. Il appartient aux populations de développer des initiatives de résilience pour soutenir les efforts déjà consentis par les plus hautes autorités », a-t-il souligné. A noter que la commune de Zoaga est située à une dizaine de kilomètres de la frontière ghanéenne. Agence d'Information du Burkina YL/SM/ata

items.title: BoG warns of regulatory action against Unlicensed Digital Credit Service Providers
items.description: The Bank of Ghana has indicated that it will soon take regulatory action against unlicensed Digital Credit Service Providers (DCSPs) operating in the country. The announcement follows the expiration of the June 30 deadline for existing mobile loan applications and Digital Credit Service Providers operating without a licence to regularise their operations. In a statement, the Bank of Ghana noted that all entities that failed to comply with the directive may be subject to regulatory action in accordance with applicable laws and regulations. The bank also advised the public to exercise caution when dealing with digital credit providers and to ensure they engage only with entities duly licensed by the Bank of Ghana. The central bank further disclosed that, in due course, it will publish and maintain an updated list of licensed Digital Credit Service Providers on its website. To support public awareness and provide further clarity on the development, the Bank of Ghana said detailed Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) have been published on its website. The FAQs are expected to provide guidance on: The licensing requirements for Digital Credit Service Providers. How to verify licensed providers. The implications for non-compliant operators and their customers. The Bank of Ghana advised members of the public to visit its official website to access the FAQs and obtain further information.

items.title: Boulgou/IPO 2026 : Les forces vives en concertation pour la réussite de la 2e édition
items.description: Boulgou/IPO 2026 : Les forces vives en concertation pour la réussite de la 2e édition Tenkodogo, 17 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Le Haut-Commissaire de la province du Boulgou, Constantin Domèbèguièrè Dabiré, a présidé le vendredi 17 juillet 2026, une rencontre de concertation dans le cadre des préparatifs de la deuxième édition de l'Immersion Patriotique Obligatoire (IPO), prévue en 2026. La rencontre a réuni les acteurs de la veille citoyenne, des boulangers, des commerçants, ainsi que les coordinations provinciale et communales des femmes. L'objectif était d'échanger sur les dispositions à prendre pour favoriser une meilleure appropriation de l'initiative par les populations de la province. À l'entame des travaux, le Haut-Commissaire a rappelé les orientations des plus hautes autorités du pays pour la réussite totale de cette deuxième édition. Il a invité les populations du Boulgou à s'approprier l'initiative en s'impliquant activement aux côtés de la cellule provinciale d'organisation. Selon lui, cet engagement devrait se traduire par une participation aux activités préparatoires, notamment celles visant à améliorer les conditions d'accueil et de séjour des appelés à Tenkodogo. Il a par ailleurs annoncé la tenue prochaine d'autres rencontres avec les différentes composantes de la société, en vue d'assurer une large diffusion de l'information et une mobilisation inclusive de toutes les couches sociales. Au terme des échanges, les participants ont salué la tenue de cette concertation et se sont engagés à s'organiser pour contribuer activement à la réussite de l'IPO 2026. Agence d'Information du Burkina SM/ata

items.title: Zidane a failli être privé des Bleus à cause de ses origines algériennes
items.description: Zinedine Zidane a failli rater une carrière exceptionnelle en équipe de France à cause de ses origines algériennes. C’est ce qu’a révélé Alain Lepeu, son premier entraîneur. Le sélectionneur des Bleuets ne l’a pas convoqué « parce qu’il est Algérien », a-t-il raconté à BFMTV. « Je lui ai dit : « Mais attends, il est né à Marseille. Tu peux le sélectionner. » C’était un samedi. Il m’a dit : « Lundi, si tu peux m’envoyer une photocopie de sa licence, je le convoquerai. » » Et c’est comme ça que la carrière en or de Zizou a commencé en équipe de France durant la saison 1987-1988. Zinedine Zidane, une carrière exceptionnelle en Bleus Dix ans après, en 1998, il offre aux Bleus sa première Coupe du monde en marquant un doublé en finale contre le Brésil (3-0) au Stade de France, avant d’enchaîner avec le titre de l’Euro 2000 face à l’Italie. Le natif de Marseille, de parents algériens originaires de Bejaïa, a mis fin à sa carrière de footballeur à l’issue d’une finale de Coupe du monde 2006 perdue face à l’Italie et marquée par son coup de boule légendaire contre l’Italien Marco Materazzi. Zinedine Zidane, qui devrait être désigné sélectionneur des Bleus avant fin juillet en remplacement de Didier Deschamps, a eu une carrière exceptionnelle comme joueur. Avec le Real Madrid, il a tout gagné, avant de devenir l’entraîneur du club le plus prestigieux au monde, en lui offrant trois Ligues des champions d’affilée (2016, 2017 et 2018). L’article Zidane a failli être privé des Bleus à cause de ses origines algériennes est apparu en premier sur TSA .

items.title: Boulgou/Béguédo : Un Conseil communal consultatif de la jeunesse mis en place
items.description: Boulgou/Béguédo : Un Conseil communal consultatif de la jeunesse mis en place Béguédo, 20 juillet 2026 (AIB) – La commune de Béguédo s'est dotée d'un Conseil communal consultatif de la jeunesse (CCCJ). Le bureau a été officiellement installé le lundi 20 juillet 2026, au cours d'une cérémonie tenue dans la salle de réunion de la mairie. La cérémonie d'installation a été présidée par le président de la délégation spéciale (PDS) de Béguédo, le camarade Moussa Wayalgba Ouédraogo. La mise en place de cette instance constitue une étape importante dans le renforcement de la participation des jeunes à la gouvernance locale. Elle a réuni de nombreux jeunes issus des associations de jeunesse ainsi que les forces vives de la commune, venus témoigner leur adhésion à cette initiative visant à promouvoir l'engagement citoyen et le développement communal. Dans son intervention, le PDS a salué la création de cette instance consultative qu'il a qualifiée de cadre privilégié de dialogue, d'union, de concertation et d'échanges entre les jeunes et les autorités communales. Il a invité les membres du bureau à accomplir avec responsabilité et abnégation les missions qui leur seront confiées. A travers ses réflexions et ses propositions, le CCCJ contribuera à renforcer l'implication des jeunes dans la gestion des affaires communales. Agence d'Information du Burkina AS/SM/ata

items.title: I will eventually switch from reggae and dancehall to highlife – Sugar Ranking
items.description: Ghanaian reggae and dancehall artiste Sugar Ranking TheRabbi has hinted at a possible shift in his musical direction. He has revealed that he may eventually put less emphasis on reggae and dancehall to focus more on highlife music. Speaking on Daybreak Hitz with Kwame Dadzie and Doreen Avio on Tuesday, July 21, 2026, the musician said highlife remains the foundation of Ghana’s musical identity and deserves greater attention from local artistes. He explained that while reggae and dancehall have played a significant role in his career, he believes there will come a time when he will dedicate more of his creativity and energy to promoting highlife. “Along the line, I will stop reggae and dancehall and continue with highlife because dancehall is a youthful genre. So far I’ve got some youthful exuberance in me I will continue to do but at a certain level, I will switch to highlife or mid-tempo reggae songs,” Sugar Ranking said. The musician, who is back to active music, disclosed that he put his promising music career on hold after joining the military, where he rose through the ranks to become a commissioned officer. According to Sugar Ranking TheRabbi, his military career demanded his full attention, leaving little room to pursue music publicly. He explained that regulations of the Ghana Armed Forces at the time did not permit serving military officers to perform publicly as musicians. This forced him to step away from the spotlight. The outspoken musician began his music career in the late 1990s and became a familiar name on Ghana’s reggae and dancehall circuit before taking a break to pursue his military career. Known for his conscious lyrics and roots reggae influences, he has over the years built a reputation as one of the genre’s veteran performers. Following his return to music, he has released songs including No Pressure, Them Go Do, Solidarity in Africa, Ajo and Grass to Grace as part of his renewed effort to reconnect with music lovers and re-establish his presence on the Ghanaian music scene. Listen to his interview on Daybreak Hitz here .

items.title: Outgoing British envoy praises Tinubu’s economic reforms
items.description: The outgoing British High Commissioner to Nigeria, Richard Montgomery, on Tuesday said President Bola Tinubu’s economic reforms would put Nigeria on a better footing in the long term. A statement by Dare Adekanmbi, Special Adviser to the Chairman of the Nigeria Revenue Service (NRS), Dr Zacch Adedeji, said the British High Commissioner spoke in Abuja during a farewell visit to the Revenue House. According to Montgomery, although the reforms might have caused short-term pain for ordinary people, they are fundamental to restoring vitality to the economy. The envoy said that being in Nigeria over a three-year period, when so much had happened for the good, was awesome. “I know that a lot of the economic reforms are tough, and they can cause short-term pain for ordinary people, you know, devaluation, inflation, and so forth. “However, I stand by and continue to brief our new Prime Minister, Andy Burnham, who’s hoping to speak to Mr President at some point in the next couple of weeks. “I know that Nigeria is complicated. I know that budget and fiscal management are always particularly difficult because of the way the budget works in the National Assembly,” he said. He said that what the country’s economic management team, which includes the NRS boss, had done to try and put Nigeria on a better economic footing had been fantastic. Montgomery described the NRS as one of the best partners of the British High Commission, and commended Adedeji for doing a “tough but good work”. Responding, the NRS chairman expressed regret that Montgomery was leaving the country at this time, while applauding the way he undertook his assignment. “The way you’ve done this work actually changed the story, most especially your support, both personal as well as the technical assistance for our reforms. “The support is not lost on us. I would also like to thank you for your commitment, and I am very grateful. “You saw us as part of the economic fabric of this country. Those engagements really went a long way in shaping our reforms,” Adedeji said. The post Outgoing British envoy praises Tinubu’s economic reforms appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Global road deaths drop 21%, but Africa records alarming rise
items.description: …crashes still kill 1.16 million yearly as WHO urges tougher laws, safer roads By Chioma Obinna A new data released by the World Health Organisation, WHO, on Tuesday revealed that road traffic deaths have fallen by 21 per cent globally over the past 15 years despite a surge in the number of vehicles on the world’s roads even as WHO warned that the gains remain fragile as Africa continues to witness rising fatalities. The report showed that road traffic deaths declined between 2011 and 2025 even as more than one billion additional vehicles were added to global roads. However, road crashes still claimed an estimated 1.16 million lives in 2025, making them one of the world’s leading public health threats. The report comes as member states adopted new United Nations General Assembly declaration committing countries to accelerate efforts towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goal of cutting road deaths and serious injuries by 50 per cent by 2030, using 2021 as the baseline. Despite the overall global improvement, the WHO African Region recorded a worrying 17 per cent increase in road deaths, making it the only region where fatalities rose significantly during the review period. Road traffic injuries also remain the leading cause of death among children and young people aged between five and 29 years. WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, described the decline in global deaths as proof that investments in safer transport systems produce measurable results but stressed that much more needs to be done. “This progress shows that change is possible when countries invest in safe road systems. In the last 15 years we’ve made real progress in reducing road deaths, despite surging vehicle numbers and major shifts in how people move,” Tedros said. “I commend countries for the strong political declaration they are adopting today, which aims to accelerate action to ensure more roads are designed for people first, not motor vehicles, with safety as priority. All road deaths are preventable and none are acceptable.” The WHO said rapid urbanisation, the explosion in motorcycle use and the emergence of new forms of mobility are reshaping transport systems globally. According to the report, the number of motorcycles worldwide more than tripled between 2011 and 2025, driven largely by the expansion of ride-hailing and delivery services. Motorcyclists now account for nearly one in every three road deaths globally. The agency noted that wearing approved motorcycle helmets can reduce the risk of death in a crash by more than six times. The report also raised concerns over the growing use of e-bikes, e-scooters and autonomous vehicles, saying governments must update regulations and safety standards to keep pace with changing transport technologies. Director of WHO’s Department of Health Determinants, Promotion and Prevention, Dr Etienne Krug, said vulnerable road users continue to bear the greatest burden of fatalities. “More than half of all road deaths are people who aren’t even in a car, they are walking, cycling or riding a motorcycle. When roads are designed as if only car drivers matter, these people are left dangerously exposed,” Krug said. “We know what works, strong laws and enforcement, safe infrastructure and vehicles with particular attention to lowering speed and addressing impaired driving. This declaration means we must all go further and faster.” Under the new UN declaration, countries pledged to establish dedicated road safety agencies, strengthen data collection, enforce tougher traffic laws, improve vehicle safety standards and invest in safer road infrastructure. The declaration also promotes the Safe System approach, which assumes that human error is inevitable and focuses on designing roads, vehicles and speed limits that minimise the risk of fatal or life-changing injuries when crashes occur. The WHO noted that progress has been uneven across regions. While the European Region recorded a 36 per cent reduction in road deaths and the Western Pacific achieved a 15 per cent decline, South-East Asia recorded only a two per cent drop, the Americas showed no improvement, and Africa experienced a sharp increase. Overall, 10 countries succeeded in halving road deaths during the decade to 2021, while more than half of all countries reported declining fatalities. WHO said strengthening road safety would not only save lives but also reduce air pollution, ease traffic congestion, promote walking and cycling, and contribute to healthier, more sustainable cities. The post Global road deaths drop 21%, but Africa records alarming rise appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Wontumi’s lawyers only sought an adjournment, not to move motions – Deputy AG
items.description: Deputy Attorney-General and Minister for Justice, Dr Justice Srem-Sai, has rejected claims that Chairman Wontumi’s legal team was prevented from arguing pending applications before the High Court before his conviction and sentencing. According to him, the lawyers who appeared in place of lead counsel, Samuel Atta Akyea, informed the court that their sole instruction was to seek an adjournment and not to move any of the pending motions. Speaking on JoyNews’ New Night on Tuesday, July 21, Dr Srem-Sai said assertions that the court denied the defence an opportunity to argue its applications did not reflect what transpired during the proceedings. “The third claim that my learned friend (counsel for Wontumi, Samuel Atta Akyea) also made, that the lawyers that they sent to the court were not allowed to move the motion. Probably, again, my senior was not in court, so he probably didn’t understand what happened. “The junior that he [Atta Akyea] sent, or his colleague lawyer that he sent, said that their instruction was to take a date, to take an adjournment. That was their instruction,” he said. He added, “When a lawyer comes to you to say that my instruction is this, what he is saying is that this is the only thing I’m asking.” The Deputy Attorney-General said the trial judge declined the request for an adjournment after finding that the explanation offered by the defence was inconsistent with the court’s schedule. According to him, although lead counsel cited a prior professional engagement abroad, the court held that “he agreed to the date, so there cannot be any prior arrangement which will conflict with this”. He also questioned claims that Wontumi had been denied his constitutional right to legal representation, noting that another lawyer from the defence team subsequently indicated he had been instructed to continue handling the case after the conviction. To Dr Srem-Sai, this demonstrated that the accused was not left without legal representation. “The lawyer who handed over to lawyer Atta Akyea said that he had been instructed to continue the case even after conviction,” he noted. The Deputy Attorney-General urged lawyers and the public to study the court’s judgement before concluding the case, arguing that the appeal process—not public commentary—was the appropriate avenue for challenging the ruling. His remarks come after counsel for Chairman Wontumi criticised the High Court’s refusal to adjourn proceedings and argued that the decision contributed to an unfair trial.

items.title: Deuxième phase du projet SANSAS: 75 structures de santé vont bénéficier de 5 milliards d’investissements
items.description: Après la première phase qui a touché 15 structures de santé, l’ONG Solthis envisage d’étendre son intervention géographique dans la région de Sédhiou. Cette volonté intervient à la suite du lancement de la deuxième phase du projet Sansas. Sédhiou – Quelques mois plus tôt, lors de la clôture du projet Santé reproductive des adolescents et jeunes du Sénégal (Sansas), piloté par l’ONG Solthis, le gouverneur de la région, Diadia Dia, plaidait pour le renforcement des interventions afin que le projet puisse toucher l’ensemble des points de prestation en matière de santé de la reproduction. Cette demande semble avoir trouvé un écho favorable, puisque l’ONG Solthis a procédé ce mardi 21 juillet au lancement de la deuxième phase du projet Sansas. Selon Christian Samabou, le responsable de l’ONG Solthis à Sédhiou, ce renouvellement ratissera plus largement en ciblant 75 structures de santé de la région de Sédhiou. « Le projet Sansas envisage aussi de faire évoluer les pratiques en renforçant davantage l’aspect genre, la santé climatique et en réduisant les vulnérabilités sociales. Toujours sur le volet social, le projet va travailler dans le sens d’impliquer les jeunes dans les instances de décision », a-t-il assuré. Jonas Souloubany BASSENE (correspondant)

items.title: Reug Reug prêt à combattre dès le mois d’août
items.description: C’est un tournant capital dans la carrière d’Oumar Kane alias Reug Reug. Le combattant sénégalais des Arts martiaux mixtes (Mma) a annoncé la fin de son aventure avec le One championship, après cinq années de collaboration. Désormais libre de tout engagement, l’ancien champion du monde des poids lourds de l’organisation affirme être prêt à relever un nouveau défi et espère effectuer son retour dans la cage dès le mois d’août. L’aventure de Reug Reug avec le One championship (ligue de sport de combat basée à Singapour) appartient désormais au passé. Le combattant sénégalais de Mma a annoncé, le samedi 18 juillet, sur ses réseaux sociaux, que son contrat avec l’organisation asiatique est officiellement terminé. La séparation intervient suite à sa défaite face au Russe Anatoly Malykhin, lors de leur revanche disputée le 15 juillet au Lumpinee stadium de Bangkok (Thaïlande). « Après cinq ans, mon chapitre avec One championship a officiellement pris fin », a écrit le pensionnaire de Thiaroye. Dans son message, Reug Reug révèle avoir lui-même sollicité sa libération, une demande finalement acceptée par l’organisation. « J’ai tout donné à cette organisation, mais après tout ce qui s’est passé avant, pendant et après mon dernier combat, je ne pouvais plus continuer dans ces circonstances », a-t-il expliqué, sans toutefois préciser les raisons exactes de son départ. Cette annonce marque la fin d’une collaboration entamée en janvier 2021. Au fil des années, le lutteur s’est imposé comme l’un des principaux mastodontes africains du Mma au sein du One championship, jusqu’à décrocher, en novembre 2024, la ceinture mondiale des poids lourds en faisant chuter Anatoly Malykhin, jusque-là invaincu. Mais cette suprématie aura été de courte durée. Le 15 juillet dernier, le Russe avait pris sa revanche en s’imposant par Ko, au quatrième round. Reug Reug avait ainsi perdu sa couronne mondiale, avant de décider, aujourd’hui, de mettre un terme à son aventure avec l’organisation asiatique. Malgré cette déconvenue qu’il n’attendait sûrement pas, l’enfant chéri de Thiaroye-sur-Mer affiche déjà ses ambitions. « Ma libération a été acceptée. Je suis prêt pour le prochain chapitre, prêt à combattre dès le mois d’août et mon prochain combat sera annoncé vendredi », a-t-il assuré, laissant entrevoir une signature imminente avec une nouvelle organisation de la discipline. Dans son message, le combattant de 34 ans a tenu à remercier tous ceux qui l’ont accompagné durant ces cinq années, promettant à ses supporters de les retrouver « prochainement dans la cage ». Il est pertinent de souligner qu’à ce jour, One Championship n’a toutefois publié aucun communiqué officiel sur son site confirmant cette séparation. L’annonce émane exclusivement de Reug Reug, même si ses déclarations laissent entendre qu’un accord a été effectivement trouvé entre les deux parties pour mettre fin à leur collaboration. Révélé au grand public du Mma lors du gala Ares 1 (Ares fighting championship), organisé le 14 décembre 2019 au Musée des Civilisations noires de Dakar, Reug Reug avait réussi des débuts remarqués en dominant le Français Sofiane Boukichou. Quelques mois plus tard, il a rejoint One Championship, où il a fait ses premiers pas, le 22 janvier 2021, à Singapour, en battant le Camerounais Alain Ngalani lors du One unbreakable II. Aujourd’hui libre de tout contrat, l’ancien champion du monde des poids lourds s’apprête à ouvrir un nouveau chapitre de sa carrière. Il reste à savoir quelle organisation accueillera celui qui demeure l’un des ambassadeurs les plus en vue du Mma africain. Par Abdoulaye DEMBÉLÉ

items.title: Borrowing to fund Free SHS sends wrong signal — Gideon Boako
items.description: The Deputy Ranking Member on Parliament’s Finance Committee and Member of Parliament for Tano North, Dr Gideon Boako, has criticised the government’s decision to finance secondary education through borrowing, arguing that such an approach demonstrates a lack of commitment to prioritising education. Contributing to parliamentary proceedings on Tuesday, Dr Boako said education should be funded primarily through domestic revenue rather than loans that would impose future repayment obligations on the country. He maintained that access to quality secondary education is too important to depend on borrowed funds. “We are looking for equitable access to education, the quality and relevance of education, strengthening secondary education systems and providing a contingency response component to enable rapid response to eligible emergencies. These components are so germane to education in this country, and for that matter if Ghana was to finance access to secondary education, I least expected that we will resort to loan to do such financing.” He questioned the message being sent to young Ghanaians, saying, “If any Ghanaian young lady or young man is listening to us today and we are telling them that we do not prioritise education such that the revenues that we generate from this country are channelled into education but rather we are going to borrow to finance secondary education, that is very sad.” Dr Boako made the remarks in Parliament while debating government financing for secondary education, urging the administration to place greater emphasis on mobilising domestic resources to sustain investments in education.

items.title: At least 41 dead in Guyana capsized ferry disaster
items.description: At least 41 people died after a ferry capsized off the coast of Guyana over the weekend, the government said Tuesday. The MV Barima was traveling from the capital Georgetown to Port Kaituma in Guyana’s inland Essequibo region when it flipped over on Saturday night. A desperate search for survivors has been underway since early Sunday, with 77 people rescued so far and dozens more missing, according to authorities. A government report released Tuesday revised the death toll from 27 to 41. “A total of 41 bodies have now been recovered,” it stated. “The first recovery for the day was made at 10:15 am (1415GMT), and 14 bodies have been retrieved as of 1 pm” Tuesday, it added. There had earlier been confusion over how many were involved in the tragedy due to an inaccurate passenger manifest which listed 133 people aboard, Public Works Minister Juan Edghill said. But on Tuesday, Prime Minister Mark Phillips said he was confident that 179 people had been on board when the accident occurred. Two crew members aboard the aging ferry tested positive for marijuana after the incident, he told reporters Sunday, adding that an investigation was underway. The ship began its journey in mid-afternoon and a distress call was received about eight hours later. The ferry was built in 1939, according to the shipping website VesselFinder. Boats typically travel by sea before reaching a river to arrive at Port Kaituma, which is located in the depths of a dense rainforest. Guyana, a small English-speaking country at the northeast corner of South America, is locked in a territorial dispute with neighboring Venezuela over the oil-rich Essequibo region. The post At least 41 dead in Guyana capsized ferry disaster appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: No legal basis for Wontumi’s conviction – Atta Akyea fights back
items.description: Samuel Atta Akyea, the lead counsel for the Ashanti Regional Chairman of the New Patriotic Party (NPP), Bernard Antwi Boasiako (Chairman Wontumi), has described his client’s conviction as unsupported by law or evidence, insisting that the defence team will challenge the ruling through the appellate courts. Speaking on JoyFM’s NewsNight on Tuesday, July 21, Mr Atta Akyea argued that a review of the prosecution’s case revealed significant gaps that could not justify the judgement. “I can tell you something which is very consequential. Looking at the poverty of the prosecution’s case, I do not see how this judgement is founded on law and evidence,” he said. Mr Atta Akyea maintained that the prosecution failed to produce direct evidence showing that Chairman Wontumi unlawfully assigned his mining concession or received any financial benefit from the alleged transaction. According to him, the prosecution witnesses never testified that the NPP chairman transferred mineral rights to them or demanded payment in exchange for access to the concession. “Today we’re talking about an assignment of a mining right. On whose mere say-so? The man has not said, ‘Give me 250,000 US dollars and I will give you the concession.’ They didn’t testify to that. They never said he wanted money from them,” he stated. He explained that the evidence before the court showed only that the individuals had been permitted to undertake land reclamation works and establish coconut plantations after the reclamation. “If the two individuals who became the prosecution’s witnesses went on illegal mining, how do you attribute their criminal activities to Chairman Wontumi?” he questioned. Read also: Chairman Wontumi sentenced to 20 years in prison The former Akim Abuakwa South Member of Parliament also criticised the prosecution’s reliance on witnesses whom he said should themselves have been facing criminal charges. “For the life of me, I can’t believe how those who were supposed to be charged were instead called to testify against the man who gave them the opportunity to reclaim the land,” he argued. Mr Atta Akyea further rejected suggestions that allowing others to work on a mining concession amounts to transferring ownership or mineral rights. “When you have a farm, and you employ people to harvest cassava or work on it, does that mean you have assigned ownership of the farm to them? The absurdity is monstrous,” he said. He insisted the conviction was based largely on circumstantial evidence rather than direct proof of any unlawful transfer of mining rights. The lawyer also criticised the trial court for dismissing constitutional issues raised by the defence, describing the decision as “an aberration of justice”. “I raised constitutional issues for the consideration of the judge, but they were ignored. We will test these matters before the appellate courts,” he said. Mr Atta Akyea confirmed that the legal team will appeal the judgement at the Court of Appeal and, if necessary, proceed to the Supreme Court. “We will exercise every right available under the law. If we are unsuccessful at the Court of Appeal, we will proceed to the Supreme Court. We will use every legal avenue available to ensure that justice is done,” he added.

items.title: L’opposition de Belloumi et Boughdiri acceptée, un procès fixé
items.description: La Cour d’appel de Tunis a accepté l’opposition formulée par les deux journalistes Hamza Belloumi et Insaf Boughdiri condmnés par défaut à un an de prison ferme. Cette décision prononcée, ce mardi 21 juillet 2026, annule de ce fait le jugement rendu en leur absence et donne lieu à un nouvel examen de l’affaire. Le procès a été fixé au 2 décembre 2026, sachant que cette affaire remonte à 2015 suite à une plainte déposée par l'ancien président Moncef Marzouki, après la diffusion d'une vidéo qui lui a été faussement attribuée, dans l'émission « Le Huitième Jour » (Al Youm Al-thamen). On notera que les deux journalistes avaient à l'époque reconnu et corrigé leur erreur avant de présenter des excuses. L’article L'opposition de Belloumi et Boughdiri acceptée, un procès fixé est apparu en premier sur Kapitalis .

items.title: Osun 2026: Aregbesola leads ADC campaign in Ife
items.description: By Shina Abubakar The National Secretary of the African Democratic Congress, ADC, Ogbeni Rauf Aregbesola, on Tuesday led the party’s governorship campaign council to Ile-Ife to kick-start its governorship campaign. Aregbesola was received by a large crowd at the Ooni’s Palace in the company of the party’s governorship candidate, Najeem Salaam, and other party chieftains. Addressing the officials led by the Lowa Adimula of Ifeland, Oba Adekola Adeyeye, Aregbesola said the choice of Ife for the take-off of the ADC campaign is symbolic and also historical. His words were, “Ile-Ife remains our source. It has been about eight years since we last came here for this purpose. We came with the candidate of our former political party, and although the people of Ife stood solidly with us, God did not permit that opportunity to be fully utilised. “Our tradition has always been to begin our political campaigns in Ile-Ife because we believe this is our source. Since 2004, we have consistently visited the palace to seek royal blessings before embarking on major political activities, and we are here today to continue that tradition.” Earlier, Oba Adeyeye reaffirmed the Ooni’s commitment to promoting peaceful coexistence, irrespective of political affiliations, while describing Ile-Ife as the spiritual and historical cradle of the Yoruba race. He stressed the need for successive governments and political leaders to accord Ile-Ife the level of attention and development befitting its historical significance. He adds, “Ile-Ife remains the source and ancestral home of the Yoruba people. Politics in modern Nigeria has a rich history that also has strong roots in Ile-Ife. The late Ooni Adesoji Aderemi served as Governor of the old Western Region, and his remarkable contributions were felt not only in Ile-Ife but throughout Yorubaland and Nigeria as a whole. “Since he left office, Ile-Ife has not experienced the level of development that befits its status as the cradle of the Yoruba race. The Hausa people regard Sokoto as their source, and today Sokoto continues to enjoy significant development. The Igbo people hold Enugu in high esteem as one of their historic centres, and its growth is evident. We, as Yoruba, who acknowledge Ile-Ife as our source, must collectively ensure that this ancient city receives the attention and development it deserves.” The post Osun 2026: Aregbesola leads ADC campaign in Ife appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Iraq’s Man in the Middle
items.description: E very Iraqi prime minister since the fall of Saddam Hussein has inherited the same impossible job: Convince Washington that Iraq is not slipping entirely into Iran’s orbit while convincing Tehran that it is not becoming an American outpost. Iraq’s new prime minister now faces that challenge under the most trying of circumstances. The United States and Iran are at war—and Iraq sits between them. When I met with Ali al-Zaidi during his visit to Washington, D.C., last week, which included an Oval Office audience with President Trump, I was looking for signs that Iraq’s strategy had changed. Instead, I heard a familiar refrain: fewer American troops; more American business. “Military presence does not create ties between societies,” he told me in a rare interview, conducted under heavy security in downtown Washington. “Economic engagement does.” It’s an aspiration that’s running up against a challenging reality. Roughly 2,000 American troops remain in his country, advising Iraqi security forces in their fight against the Islamic State. Meanwhile, dozens of Iran-backed militias operate across Iraq, despite repeated government promises to bring them under state control. As of now, the U.S. troops are set to leave by the end of September, bringing a symbolic end to America’s war in Iraq after 23 years. Many experts I spoke with are skeptical, however, noting that if fighting continues to escalate between Washington and Tehran after the collapse of the recent cease-fire, those troops are strategically positioned to bolster America’s Middle Eastern presence—even if they are also among the most exposed to attack. Around the time al-Zaidi and I spoke in Washington, three U.S. service members were reported dead in incidents in Jordan and Iraq, demonstrating how quickly Iraq can once again become a focal point of conflict. Trump “had the inclination to deliver a severe blow” to Iran after days of tit-for-tat strikes, al-Zaidi told me, though he declined to elaborate on what that might entail, or whether it involved ground troops. He would say only that Trump had asked for his opinion, and he advised to delay retaliation. “Trump expressed his frustration, saying that despite the agreement reached with the Iranians, they failed to honor it—a sentiment echoed by the Iranians regarding the U.S.,” he said. “You can always resort to force later, but negotiation is what matters.” (A U.S. official didn’t address the exchange between Trump and al-Zaidi, but said in a statement that “these devastating blows will continue until the President deems otherwise.” The official added that talks between the U.S. and Iran continue.) Over the past two decades, Iran has ratcheted up its influence in Iraq through political parties, economic ties, and armed groups affiliated with its Popular Mobilization Forces. The U.S., meanwhile, provides military training and intelligence support against the remnants of ISIS, and plays a key role in Iraq’s access to the global financial system through the dollar-based banking network. U.S. officials told me Iraqi intelligence played a key role in the U.S. operation that killed Iranian General Qassem Soleimani outside the airport in Baghdad in January 2020, a military achievement from Trump’s first term that he still celebrates. (Iraq has denied any involvement.) Yet that strike triggered a crisis in U.S.-Iraq relations, fueling demands from powerful Iraqi factions to expel American forces altogether. [ Read: Inside the decision to kill Iran’s Qassem Soleimani ] Iraq matters to Washington for reasons that extend far beyond Baghdad. American troops stationed there provide logistical and intelligence support to Iraqi security forces, making their bases frequent targets whenever tensions escalate with Iran. Every escalation raises the same question: Can Baghdad disarm the militias, most of which take their orders from Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps? Al-Zaidi repeatedly insisted, his voice rising with anger, that “there is no option except surrendering weapons.” He is not prepared to accept a failed state. On almost every issue with Iraq that has frustrated Washington for two decades—corruption, militias, sovereignty, and Iranian influence—al-Zaidi claimed the situation was changing. Yet each pledge echoed promises made by nearly every Iraqi prime minister before him. He acknowledged as much, but insisted that some of his early crackdowns on corrupt public servants have succeeded, indicating that this time is different. A t 41 years old, al-Zaidi is the youngest Iraqi prime minister. A political outsider and multimillionaire businessman, he belongs to a generation shaped by the wars that remade modern Iraq. He was born during the Iran-Iraq War of the 1980s, when Saddam sought to take advantage of the political chaos in Iran following its 1979 Islamic Revolution. Saddam ordered an invasion of Iran the following year on the assumption that Tehran’s military had been depleted by the domestic upheaval. But Iraqi forces faced stiff Iranian resistance, and the war dragged on for about eight years, killing hundreds of thousands of soldiers on both sides and devastating both economies. Today’s confrontation, between the United States and Israel on one side and Iran on the other, has made commercial shipping and global energy supplies central targets, with oil tankers again caught in the crossfire, as they were 40 years ago. When I asked al-Zaidi what Iraq had learned from its own war with Iran, his answer was striking: “Even the winner loses,” he said, while taking a drag from his vape. [ Read: The forever negotiation ] The answer reflected Iraq’s experience. The Iran-Iraq War ended without a decisive victor but left Iraq heavily indebted, politically weakened, and socially scarred. Those burdens helped set the stage for Saddam’s invasion of Kuwait in August 1990, which triggered the Gulf War and, ultimately, the chain of events that culminated in the 2003 U.S. invasion of Iraq, when al-Zaidi was 18 years old. The removal of Saddam by the U.S. was followed by the rise of militias with deep ideological, security, and economic dependence on Tehran. Iraq, al-Zaidi told me, should be “a meeting point, not a place for hostility” between the U.S. and Iran. His country can bridge the divide by getting U.S. forces to leave and then by ensuring that any rogue militia groups disarm. He insisted that economic opportunity is the answer to Iraq’s problems: If people feel they have a future, they won’t turn to warfare or sectarianism. He acknowledged he will likely face resistance and said he could die trying to make it happen. “We will accept nothing less,” he told me. Throughout our conversation, which was in Arabic, al-Zaidi sounded less like a career politician than a chief executive pitching a turnaround strategy. Iraq has resources. America has capital and technology. Replace soldiers with investors, and the relationship becomes sustainable. But signs of recovery last year have been snuffed out by the latest conflict, which has disrupted trade routes, strained public finances, and prompted the International Monetary Fund to forecast that Iraq’s economy will shrink by 6.8 percent this year. Al-Zaidi pointed to new energy agreements signed in Washington as evidence that investors should look beyond the war. But he also acknowledged that his country’s economy is suffering greatly from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, losing about $50 billion in revenue because of halted oil exports, he said. Al-Zaidi emerged as a compromise candidate for prime minister in late April after months of political deadlock. He has vowed to forgo a government salary, avoid forming a political party, and serve only one four-year term. Since taking office, he has paired those promises with high-profile anti-corruption raids, including the June arrest of dozens of officials, among them the deputy oil minister, inside Baghdad’s Green Zone, the heavily fortified district in central Baghdad now controlled by the Iraqi government. Before politics, he built a business empire spanning agriculture, logistics, and energy. A bank where he served as chairman later lost access to U.S.-dollar transactions after American authorities raised concerns over alleged Iran-linked money laundering. Trump, who described al-Zaidi as “handsome” when they met last week, appears receptive to a leader who speaks in the language of investment rather than ideology. Al-Zaidi pitches Iraq, first and foremost, as a business opportunity, though he avoids the term deal , saying investment will naturally follow because the two countries want to work together. “We are discussing an investment partnership between Iraq and the U.S. that would span 30 years,” he added. “The fighting could cease in months, days, or even hours—that rests with the politicians.” [ Read: ‘We may sleepwalk our way back to war’ ] Al-Zaidi’s own rise to power reflects Washington’s enduring influence. After months of political paralysis, former Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki, an influential hard-liner with close ties to Iran, was pushed aside earlier this year after Trump publicly threatened to cut U.S. assistance if al-Maliki returned to office. Al-Zaidi emerged as the alternative because he was acceptable to Iraq’s competing political factions and, just as important, was unlikely to face resistance from Washington. His challenge now is to prove that political compromise can translate into actual authority over a state where many of the most heavily armed actors still operate outside government control. Al-Zaidi has pledged that by September 30, independent armed factions will either surrender their weapons or be absorbed into Iraq’s official security forces. The coming months will determine whether al-Zaidi’s vision survives contact with the region’s oldest reality: Iraq rarely gets to choose the wars that shape its future.

items.title: Fake doctor nabbed in Calabar over N500,000 UCTH job scam
items.description: By Ike Uchechukwu, CALABAR A 39-year-old man, Udeme Emmanuel Ekpeme, has been arrested by the Cross River State Police Command for allegedly posing as a medical doctor and swindling a woman of Five Hundred Thousand Naira with a promise of securing hospital jobs for her children. The suspect was picked up by operatives attached to the Uwanse Division in Calabar South LGA following a petition by Mrs. Edemawan Samuel, 45, who accused him of parading himself as a doctor and taking the money from her under the pretext of helping her children gain employment at the University of Calabar Teaching Hospital (UCTH). Police say preliminary findings show the suspect exploited his false identity to gain the complainant’s trust before defrauding her. Exhibits linked to the case have since been recovered, and he remains in custody as investigations continue. Confirming the arrest, the Police Public Relations Officer, ASP Sunday Eitokpah , said the Commissioner of Polucee had ordered a full-scale investigation into the matter. “The Commissioner of Police has directed that this case be thoroughly investigated to establish the true circumstances surrounding it and to determine whether there are other victims, “The suspect will be charged to court as soon as sufficient evidence is established,” Eitokpah said. He added that detectives were widening the probe to unravel the suspect’s full activities. “We are conducting a discreet investigation to uncover the extent of his dealings and identify any other victims or accomplices,” he said He further assured residents that the command would keep the public updated as the case progresses. The post Fake doctor nabbed in Calabar over N500,000 UCTH job scam appeared first on Vanguard News .
